checks.a_analysis: The output JSON accurately reflects the textual content of the source PDF. No paragraphs, sentences, or table entries have been invented. The captions and summaries for the tables are expected AI additions for accessibility and do not constitute fabrication. The structure of splitting the table into two parts follows the physical layout of the source pages.
The source document contains no phone numbers, email addresses, mailing addresses, or URLs. Consequently, no such information appears in the output JSON.

The output JSON contains two table objects corresponding to the content on Page 1 and Page 2 of the source document. Every row listed in the 'Framework Layers' and 'Other Important Layers' sections is present in the output. The continuation of the 'Wetlands' description from page 1 to page 2 is handled correctly.

I counted the rows in both source and output. 
Page 1 source: 9 framework rows + 18 'Other Important' rows = 27 data rows. Output Table 1 contains 2 header rows and 27 data rows. 
Page 2 source: 1 continuation row + 10 new data rows = 11 rows. Output Table 2 contains 11 data rows. 
All rows are accounted for.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Text content within the description cells matches the source accurately. For example, technical descriptions like the SSURGO database or the NPDES definition are captured in full without omission. The split of the 'Wetlands' description across the two tables is done faithfully according to the document's layout.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: There are no meaningful differences in wording or punctuation that would alter the information conveyed. All terminology (e.g., 'TINs?', '1% annual chance', 'ETJs') matches the source exactly.

checks.a_analysis: The output does not contain any duplicated content. The title is used as the section title and within the captions of the two tables generated from the two pages, which is an expected restructuring for accessibility rather than content duplication. Every data row from the source appears exactly once in the JSON.

The heading hierarchy is consistent. The document title is mapped to a section title (H1 equivalent). As the document consists entirely of tables under this single heading, there are no hierarchy gaps or incorrectly nested headings.

The table structure is correctly preserved. The conversion accurately identifies the headers at the top of the first page ('Framework Layers', 'Description') and the sub-header row in the middle of page 1 ('Other Important Layers', 'Description'). The page break is handled by creating two table objects, and the 'Wetlands' row which is split across pages 1 and 2 is faithfully transcribed as two separate rows (one in each table), maintaining the visual organization of the source.
The reading order follows the logical flow of the source document, moving from the title into the table content and proceeding row-by-row through the two pages.

There are no formal lists in the source document. Elements that are conceptually lists within table cell descriptions (e.g., in the 'Surface Waters' description) are correctly rendered as string content within the table cells, matching the source.
The source document contains no hyperlinks, and no links have been added to the output JSON.

checks.a_analysis: In the source PDF, the 'Other Important Layers' header appears midway through the first page, correctly categorizing the layers starting with 'Land Use'. In the rendered HTML output, this header has been moved to the second row of the table, immediately following the 'Framework Layers' header. This disrupts the visual organization and flow of the document, as the 'Framework Layers' (Orthophotography through Elevation) are now visually preceded by the 'Other Important Layers' header, which is incorrect.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF on page 1 shows the header 'Other Important Layers' and 'Description' appearing after the row for 'Elevation'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows 'Other Important Layers' and 'Description' as the second row of the table, located at the top of the first table block.

checks.a_analysis: The tables in the HTML output are rendered correctly with visible borders and aligned text. While the table is split across two HTML components due to the PDF page break (resulting in the 'Wetlands' row being split), the content remains readable and understandable. The misplacement of the header row is addressed in check V1.
The source PDF document contains no images; it is entirely composed of text and tables. Therefore, no images are missing or broken in the output.

The document title and section headings appear in both the main header area and the body content, which is an expected and acceptable practice for accessible document design and navigation landmarks. No actual body content paragraphs or table data rows are duplicated.
